seoTitle: How to Get Trello API Keys and Tokens
metaDescription: Step-by-step guide to generating a Trello API key and access token through the Atlassian Developer portal for custom scripts and integrations.
intro: Connecting a custom script, webhook, or backend integration to Trello starts with generating an API key and an authorized token from the Atlassian Developer portal. It's a quick process once you know where the credentials panel is hidden. This guide walks through logging in, generating your key, requesting a token, and storing both securely for use in your project.

steps.heading: Log in and open the developer portal
steps.body: Sign in to your Trello account, then navigate to the Atlassian Developer portal and accept the developer terms to proceed.

steps.heading: Generate your API key
steps.body: Click the button to generate a new API key suite, then copy the long string that appears so you can use it in your integration.

steps.heading: Request an authorized token
steps.body: Find the manual token generation link near your API key field, open it, and review the requested app permissions before continuing.

steps.heading: Authorize and copy your token
steps.body: Adjust the scope of access if needed, click Allow, then copy the generated secret token and save it somewhere secure.

steps.heading: Test and store your credentials
steps.body: Add your API key and token to your project's environment variables, then run a quick test call to confirm the connection is pulling data correctly.

faq.question: Is a Trello API key free to generate?
faq.answer: Yes, generating a Trello API key and token through the Atlassian Developer portal is free.

faq.question: Can I set an expiration on my token?
faq.answer: Yes, when generating a token you can typically choose between a short expiration window or a longer-lived option depending on your permission needs.

faq.question: What permissions does my token need?
faq.answer: It depends on your integration — read-only access is enough for pulling board data, while read and write access is needed if your script will create or update cards.

faq.question: How do I revoke a token I no longer need?
faq.answer: You can revoke active tokens from your account's developer dashboard, which immediately cuts off that credential's access.

faq.question: Why should I store keys in environment variables instead of my code?
faq.answer: Keeping credentials in environment variables rather than hardcoding them helps prevent your secret strings from being exposed if you share or publish your code.
